title: Bret Gives Up The Dream

jokes.text: Bret reveals the groceries he found were on the street — someone didn't want them
jokes.setup: Bret arrives with groceries and presents them proudly — a sandwich, jam, other items. The slow reveal of enthusiasm builds expectation that he scored a deal.
jokes.punchline: It emerges in stages: 'It was free.' 'How?' 'I found it.' 'Where?' 'It was on the street. Someone didn't want it.' Each answer is more damning than the last, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The multi-stage reveal is beautifully structured — the show withholds the full truth across several exchanges, each beat worse than the last. The deadpan delivery from Bret, treating street garbage as a legitimate grocery haul, is pure character comedy. The rhythm of the dialogue (short Q&A pairs) controls the pacing expertly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Found groceries on the street - 'Someone didn't want it'

jokes.text: Jemaine starts to spit out the street sandwich, then decides to just eat it anyway
jokes.setup: Jemaine's initial reaction to learning the sandwich came from the street is to spit it out — the normal human response.
jokes.punchline: 'I was gonna spit it out, but I think I'll just eat it. We're poor, man.' The resignation is complete. No drama, just pragmatism.
jokes.explanation: Economy is exceptional — 'I was gonna spit it out, but I think I'll just eat it' is a complete comic arc in one sentence. The follow-up 'We're poor, man / We are poor, aren't we?' is a lovely coda of mutual recognition. It earns the song that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I was gonna spit it out, but I think I'll just eat it. We're poor, man.

jokes.text: SONG: 'Inner City Pressure' — in its entirety as a comic set piece
jokes.setup: The duo's poverty is established by the street sandwich. The song is a parody of dramatic urban-poverty anthems (specifically Pet Shop Boys' 'West End Girls') applied to the comically small-scale misery of two New Zealand musicians in NYC.
jokes.punchline: The song works as a sustained escalation of petty hardships rendered in the grandiose language of social commentary. Individual lyrical jokes include: 'your toothbrush jar and a camera phone,' 'your favorite jersey is covered in lint,' 'you want to sit down but you've sold your chair / so you just stand there,' 'counting coins at the 7-Eleven from a quarter past 6 to a quarter to 7,' 'the manager Bevan starts to abuse me / hey man I just want some muesli,' 'considering secondhand underpants,' 'you think maybe you'll be a prostitute / just to pay for your lessons you're learning the flute / ladies wouldn't pay you very much for this / looks like you'll never be a concert flautist,' 'you just stay home and play synthesizers.'
jokes.explanation: This is the standout comedic achievement of the episode and one of the show's signature pieces. The gap between the grandiose Pet Shop Boys pastiche and the microscopic scale of problems (lint, muesli, a manager named Bevan) is perfectly calibrated. The rhyme scheme forces unexpected wordplay ('flute/flautist', 'muesli/abuse me') that is simultaneously terrible and inspired. Each verse escalates: jersey lint → secondhand underpants → prostitution to fund flute lessons. The bathos is structural, not random. 'You just stand there' repeated as the climactic lyric of a verse about selling your chair is comic genius. This is comparable to the 9.5 anchor — it's culturally embedded FotC material that fans quote constantly.

dialogue.line: Your toothbrush jar and a camera phone

jokes.text: 'The manager, Bevan, starts to abuse me / Hey man, I just want some muesli'
jokes.setup: The song moves to a 7-Eleven scene of coin-counting desperation
jokes.punchline: The crisis at the 7-Eleven is that the manager Bevan is hassling him... because he wants muesli. In a 7-Eleven. At 6:45am.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Bevan' (a very New Zealand/Australian name, deeply unhip) and the specificity of 'muesli' (health food, slightly earnest, not what you'd expect someone to want at a 7-Eleven) is brilliant character comedy. The rhyme 'abuse me / muesli' is perfectly terrible in the best way. Justifies 8+ — the cultural specificity makes this transcend generic poverty humor.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You want to sit down but you've sold your chair

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So you... you just stand there

jokes.text: 'Check your mind, how'd it get so bad? / What happened to those other underpants you had?'
jokes.setup: The song asks a grand existential question about poverty
jokes.punchline: The existential inquiry resolves into: what happened to your old underpants specifically?
jokes.explanation: This is the song's best callback — the grand 'check your mind' introspection collapses into a mundane follow-up question about the fate of specific underpants. The absurdist specificity ('those other underpants you had') is perfectly calibrated. Justifies 8.3 — the structure of philosophical setup → mundane punchline is executed flawlessly and the quotability is extremely high.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, man, I just want some muesli

jokes.text: Murray reveals their entire band fund is $4 because he spent $6 on a box to hold it
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine ask Murray for band funds. He says there's about $4 in the box.
jokes.punchline: 'I thought we had $10?' 'This box cost $6.' 'What was wrong with the bag?' 'The bag was useless, Bret. It had a great big hole in it. Must have lost about $20 out of that bag.' The box he bought to hold their money costs more than all the money they have.
jokes.explanation: Pure Murray — catastrophically impractical financial management delivered with bureaucratic pride ('I chose it'). The revelation that the bag also lost $20 through the hole is a perfect extension: the solution (box) was prompted by a problem that itself destroyed more money than they now have. Character-integrated to the point where no other character could do this bit.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Considering secondhand underpants

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What happened to those other underpants you had?

jokes.text: Murray calculates his 12% management cut of $4.27, arriving at '51.24 cents'
jokes.setup: Murray gives Bret and Jemaine the $4. He then mentions he's owed 12% of that.
jokes.punchline: He gets out a calculator and announces: '51.24 cents.' Bret: 'We can't even divide—' Murray: 'Just leave it.' The bureaucratic diligence applied to an amount under a dollar, producing a mathematically impossible sub-cent result, is perfect.
jokes.explanation: Murray taking his cut so seriously that he actually does the arithmetic — and the result is both mathematically wrong (12% of ~$4.27 is ~$0.51, not 51.24 cents, which are the same thing stated absurdly) and practically useless — is perfectly in character. 'Just leave it' is the right button on it. Justifies the 8 on character integration: this is Murray to his core.

dialogue.line: So you think maybe you'll be a prostitute
Just to pay for your lessons, you're learning the flute

jokes.text: Murray offers his last $50, immediately hedges 'Till Thursday, I mean,' then reveals he's owed money by the band's mousepad disaster
jokes.setup: Murray, moved by guilt, offers his personal last $50.
jokes.punchline: The T-shirts sold two. No mousepads. Murray: 'I told you — terrible idea. I've got so many mousepads at home because of your bad idea, Bret. I've lined Toby's kennel with them. He keeps eating them. I tried to waterproof the kitchen.'
jokes.explanation: The mousepad accumulation gag is strong: the specificity of 'lined Toby's kennel with them / he keeps eating them' and 'tried to waterproof the kitchen' shows real comic imagination about what a desperate person does with hundreds of useless mousepads. Murray's resentment toward Bret for the idea while simultaneously having implemented it himself is good character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Ladies wouldn't pay you very much for this

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Looks like you'll never be a concert flautist

jokes.text: Greg from the office walks in wearing the band's T-shirt, proves everyone knows Murray manages a band during office hours, then declines a mousepad
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to maintain the fiction that he's a professional government employee meeting 'businessmen.'
jokes.punchline: Greg walks in wearing the band's T-shirt: 'Everybody knows you manage a band during office hours, Murray.' Then Murray immediately tries to sell him a mousepad. 'No thanks, Murray.' Murray: 'See? Hopeless.'
jokes.explanation: The T-shirt as visual proof of the band's reach (one person in the building wears it, and he's a colleague) is an efficient piece of world-building comedy. The mousepad callback lands because Murray's inability to stop trying to monetize them even in defeat is character-consistent. 'See? Hopeless' directed at the band rather than his own sales pitch is a nice inversion.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ladies wouldn't pay you very much for this

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Looks like you'll never be a concert flautist

jokes.text: The sign-holding job interview: 'Sounds like something a lamppost could do'
jokes.setup: Eddie explains that the job is holding a sign and pointing it in the right direction, asking if that sounds doable.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine deadpans: 'Sounds like something a lamppost could do.' Eddie immediately pivots: 'You're sign material. This guy overthinks things.'
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Jemaine's observation is objectively correct (a lamppost IS functionally equivalent), which makes it funnier than a typical insult. Eddie's response — categorizing accurate logic as 'overthinking' and preferring Bret's silent compliance — is a sharp satirical swipe at low-wage labor management. 'You have what I like to call... the wrong attitude' is an elegant structural mirror.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 50 brushes now. Pretty impressive, huh?

jokes.text: Boss: 'You cannot overthink in this business' — about sign-holding
jokes.setup: Boss dismisses Jemaine's perfectly valid concern about wind
jokes.punchline: Calling Jemaine 'Enrico Fermi' for wondering what happens if wind takes the sign — treating a sign-holder's philosophical concern as dangerous over-intellectualism
jokes.explanation: The 'Enrico Fermi' namecheck elevates what could be a flat dismissal — invoking a nuclear physicist when rebuking a sign-holder's wind question is perfectly calibrated absurdity. The boss's misplaced authority and corporate-speak about sign-holding is funny throughout.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: '$4? I thought we had $10?'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'This box cost $6.'

jokes.text: Jemaine debates 'booties' vs. 'boobies' for the song lyric, concluding 'booties' makes him think of little woolen baby boots
jokes.setup: Jemaine is working on a hip-hop song late at night. He asks Bret whether 'shake your booties' or 'shake your boobies' is the better lyric.
jokes.punchline: 'Cause when I think of shake your booties, I think of those little boots that babies wear. You know, those little woolen boots that they wear.' He repeats the baby boot image multiple times with increasing specificity and sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific: Jemaine's earnest literalism about slang ('booties' as baby footwear) reads as both genuine naivety and comic self-awareness. The repetition of 'woolen boots' with full sincerity is what makes it — he's not performing confusion, he really means it. This is pure Conchords DNA: non-Anglophone innocence meets American urban vernacular.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The bag was useless, Bret. It had a great big hole in it. Must have lost about $20 out of that bag.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Yeah, shut up, Bret.' — response to Bret saying he needs to work on his people skills
jokes.setup: Bret got the sign job; Jemaine didn't
jokes.punchline: Bret's advice about people skills is immediately answered with 'shut up' — demonstrating the exact deficit being named
jokes.explanation: Short, structurally perfect — the punchline IS the demonstration of the setup's premise. Maximum economy. The joke would score higher but it's a fairly standard sitcom beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Murray calculating his 12% management fee from $4.27 and getting confused

jokes.text: Bret arrives late to band practice; Jemaine has designated his own 'free time' and 'leisure activities' — sitting in a chair, then light reading
jokes.setup: Bret arrives late and asks if they can do more practice. Jemaine says it's now 7pm, his free time, and he has leisure activities planned.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm gonna sit down over there for a bit and then I'm gonna do some light reading.' The activities Jemaine has scheduled for his leisure time are the most baseline-human possible — sitting, then reading — described with the gravity of a packed social calendar.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the formality of the announcement — 'leisure activities' for doing almost nothing — and Jemaine's absolute moral seriousness about protecting his 4 hours of earned free time. Then the visual beat of him just... sitting in the chair, doing nothing, while Bret stands watching, is perfect deadpan physical staging.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It is my last $50. Till Thursday, I mean, you know. Getting paid again, but...

jokes.text: Visual beat: Jemaine sitting in the chair doing nothing during his 'leisure activities' while Bret watches
jokes.setup: Jemaine has announced he will 'sit down over there for a bit' as his leisure activity.
jokes.punchline: He is shown doing exactly that — sitting perfectly still in the chair, in silence, as if this is a legitimate and satisfying activity. The extended hold on this image IS the joke.
jokes.explanation: The economy score is near-perfect because the joke is purely visual — no words needed to make it land. The payoff to 'I'm going to sit down over there for a bit' being a literal, sustained shot of him doing exactly that, seriously, is everything. Economy of 9.5 because there are zero words — the absence of dialogue IS the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got so many mousepads at home because of your bad idea, Bret

jokes.text: Murray holds an emergency band meeting on the public sidewalk where Bret is working — 'You're not working, you're just holding a sign'
jokes.setup: Bret tells the others to leave because he's at work. They insist it's a band meeting.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine: 'You're not working, you're just holding a sign.' This is factually accurate in a way that makes Bret unable to argue, even though it's also his job.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke that works on the lamppost logic established earlier (joke 8) — 'holding a sign' is both a job and not really work in any dignified sense. The line lands but doesn't surprise.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've lined Toby's kennel with them. He keeps eating them. I tried to waterproof the kitchen.

jokes.text: Murray calls a sidewalk band meeting with formal roll call: 'Bret. Present. Jemaine. Present. Murray, present.'
jokes.setup: Murray is holding an impromptu meeting on a public sidewalk.
jokes.punchline: He takes attendance as if it were a formal committee meeting, including calling his own name and responding to himself.
jokes.explanation: The bureaucratic formality applied to chaotic circumstances is Murray's defining characteristic, and calling his own name + responding to himself ('Murray, present') is the peak of that bit. Short and clean.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've just got a couple of businessmen here

jokes.text: Murray's Australia grudge: 'The Australians, as always, have got their stall right next to the New Zealand stall... we're gonna blow those Aussies out of the water. Bloody showoffs.'
jokes.setup: Murray describes the Trade and Migrant Expo as an opportunity.
jokes.punchline: His excitement is entirely about beating Australia, a long-running national grudge rendered in miniature. 'Bloody showoffs' is delivered as a genuine grievance.
jokes.explanation: The NZ/Australia rivalry is a recurring world-building element of the show. Murray's personal investment in beating Australians at a minor trade expo — treating it like an Olympic event — is consistent characterization. 'Bloody showoffs' is punchy and specific enough to be memorable.

dialogue.line: Everybody knows you manage a band during office hours, Murray.

jokes.text: The chicken-and-egg circular argument about gigs vs. jobs, culminating in Murray calling Bret 'a bad egg'
jokes.setup: Murray says Bret must quit his job for the gig. Bret points out he only got the job because there were no gigs. Murray says he can't get gigs if Bret has a job.
jokes.punchline: The argument loops perfectly three times before Jemaine introduces the chicken-and-egg metaphor. Murray: 'That's irrelevant, isn't it? It's stupid. The chicken, obviously.' Jemaine: 'Well, where did the chicken come from?' Murray: 'Well, it came from the— Oh.' Then Murray pivots: 'You're the egg. You're a bad egg, all right? You've derailed this meeting with another obscure comment.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural comedy. The circular Bret/Murray argument is a perfect comedy-of-logic loop (each iteration identical, with rising frustration), and Jemaine introducing 'chicken-and-egg' as a metaphor works because it IS a perfect metaphor — and Murray momentarily gets caught by the actual riddle mid-sentence ('it came from the— Oh'). Murray's recovery — 'You're a bad egg' — is the Murray equivalent of 'I knew that' plus the accusation of derailment when he is the one who just got publicly stumped is pitch-perfect. One of the best constructed scenes in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I wear that T-shirt all the time.

jokes.text: Bret's solution: 'I could just record my part on tape, I guess, and Jemaine could play along' — on a cassette
jokes.setup: Bret can't make the gig because of work. He offers an alternative.
jokes.punchline: Murray: 'Tape? A cassette tape?' The specificity of 'cassette' — this anachronistic, lo-fi solution — and Murray's horrified repetition of it plants the seed for the entire second-act payoff.
jokes.explanation: This joke works mainly in retrospect — it's the setup for the entire tape-performance subplot. Murray's appalled 'A cassette tape?' establishes the absurdity that the show then fully commits to, including the tape actually working better than Bret. High rewatch bonus for this reason.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Sounds like something a lamppost could do

jokes.text: Murray evaluating the hot dog cart's sauce-to-price ratio mid-emergency band meeting
jokes.setup: An urgent logistical dispute is happening about the gig.
jokes.punchline: Murray spots a hot dog vendor and abruptly pivots: 'Those hot dogs any good? ... $2. ... How many sauces? Two. You'd expect three sauces for that, wouldn't you?' Complete non sequitur delivered with total earnestness.
jokes.explanation: Murray's inability to stay focused on anything — and his completely misplaced consumer concerns about condiment ratio — is quintessential character comedy. The specificity of 'you'd expect three sauces for that' is funnier than any generic distraction could be. It implies Murray has strong views on hot dog sauce standards.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: You're sign material. This guy overthinks things... You cannot.

jokes.text: Sign boss Eddie's safety speech: 'I'm sorry about the particles. Don't breathe 'em in. We don't know what they are. I would suggest quick, short breaths.'
jokes.setup: Eddie addresses his sign-holding crew before their shift.
jokes.punchline: He warns them about mysterious airborne particles with the solution of 'quick, short breaths' — which is not medically different from normal breathing and provides no actual protection.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Eddie's authoritative, responsible-sounding tone delivering completely useless safety information. 'We don't know what they are' followed by a confident remedy ('quick, short breaths') is peak absurdist bureaucracy. A minor but sharply written moment.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: What if a wind comes and blows it away? You ever think of that, Enrico Fermi?

jokes.text: Eddie promotes Bret from 'Jewelry Sale' to 'Men's Suits' to 'Phones' in rapid succession, treating sign assignments as a career ladder
jokes.setup: Eddie assigns signs to his workers with the gravity of corporate portfolio management.
jokes.punchline: Bret is promoted through three different sign assignments in under a minute, each treated as a significant advancement. 'People who hold signs go on to hold many things.'
jokes.explanation: 'People who hold signs go on to hold many things' is an excellent line — it sounds like genuine inspirational management speak, the comedic gap being that 'holding things' is not an actual career trajectory. Eddie's earnestness about sign-holding as a vocation is the best running gag in the subplot.

dialogue.line: Bret's confusion about 'booties' vs 'boobies' - thinking of baby boots

jokes.text: Sign boss's motivational speech: 'People who hold signs go on to hold many things'
jokes.setup: Boss is assigning Bret to train Coco
jokes.punchline: His career-ladder pitch for sign-holding is 'people who hold signs go on to hold many things' — a profound truth that is also completely meaningless
jokes.explanation: The self-help corporate speech applied to sign-holding is a consistent source of comedy in this boss character. 'Hold many things' is genuinely quotable — it sounds like wisdom and is completely hollow. Economy score is high — six words doing substantial work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, shut up, Bret

jokes.text: Bret tells Coco 'I'm in a band' — she says 'Cool' with zero inflection, then immediately asks his name wrong ('Brit?')
jokes.setup: Bret is showing Coco the ropes of sign-holding. He drops that he's in a band, presumably hoping it impresses her.
jokes.punchline: 'Cool.' Pause. 'What's your name again?' Complete non-interest in the band revelation, followed by mishearing his name twice, hearing it a third time and saying 'It just sounds like Brit.'
jokes.explanation: The 'cool' is the joke — Bret's attempt to be interesting to Coco deflates instantly. The name-mishearing extension ('Brit? Brit like... Brittany?') is a well-observed New Zealand accent comedy bit that doesn't overstay its welcome.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Jemaine's elaborate free time schedule - sitting and light reading

jokes.text: 'There's Vikings there, right?' — Coco about New Zealand
jokes.setup: Coco says she heard New Zealand is beautiful
jokes.punchline: Her knowledge of New Zealand is that it has Vikings. Bret just says 'Eh, yeah.'
jokes.explanation: Vikings in New Zealand is a completely random wrong answer. But Bret's 'Eh, yeah' — choosing not to correct her, just to go along — is equally funny. His resignation at American ignorance of New Zealand is already established as a character trait and this is a nice expression of it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're not working, you're just holding a sign.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: This is my work. Get out of here.

jokes.text: Bret's escalating description of Coco: 'She's so hot she's making me sexist'
jokes.setup: Bret is clearly attracted to Coco. He describes her attractiveness in increasingly strained metaphors.
jokes.punchline: 'She's like a curry. I want to tell her how hot she is but she'll think I'm being sexist. She's so hot she's making me sexist. Bitch.'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. 'She's so hot she's making me sexist' is a genuine comic insight — the idea that attraction itself is somehow her fault, which Bret recognizes is unreasonable even as he believes it — is sharp and original. The logic progression (she's hot → I want to say so → she'll think I'm sexist → therefore she's making me sexist → 'Bitch') is a perfect little reductio ad absurdum. The final 'Bitch' directed at his own infatuation lands as a punchline because it's so tonally out of nowhere while being completely emotionally honest. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: SONG: 'Boom' — Jemaine's hip-hop song in which all sexual/exciting content is replaced with the word 'boom'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has been composing a hip-hop track. The song attempts to be sexually suggestive urban music.
jokes.punchline: Every noun, verb, and euphemism is replaced with 'boom' or 'boom boom,' escalating to the point where the lyrics are purely boom-words: 'Then we boom boom boom boom boom boom boom.' The 'Fast forward selector!' command mid-song is a comedic stage direction acknowledging the song is going on too long. 'Bret's the boom king' as the finale.
jokes.explanation: The 'Boom' song works because the premise commits completely — by the end, even the word 'boom' itself has become meaningless through repetition, which is both the joke and a sort of accidental commentary on genre. The 'Fast forward selector!' meta-interruption is brilliant — it's the show acknowledging the joke's logic while extending it. Character-integrated: only Jemaine, a sincere but culturally displaced New Zealander, would produce this as serious hip-hop. The callback to the booties/boobies discussion (this IS the song that resulted from that debate) adds resonance.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The chicken, obviously.
Well, where did the chicken come from?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Murray's New Zealand expo pitch descends into the toothbrush fence: 'A whole fence made out of toothbrushes. Over 50 toothbrushes.'
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to attract visitors to the New Zealand stall. He lists attractions.
jokes.punchline: After 'beautiful scenery' and 'bungee jumping,' Murray lands on the toothbrush fence — the same one he was reading about in his office — as a flagship attraction. 'Imagine that, a whole fence made out of toothbrushes. Over 50 toothbrushes.'
jokes.explanation: The callback to Murray's earlier toothbrush fence reading pays off beautifully — it establishes that Murray considers this genuinely impressive, and placing it as a climactic tourism attraction (after scenery and bungee jumping) reveals that Murray's scale for impressive things is wildly miscalibrated. 'Imagine that' as a genuine invitation to be awed lands perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're the egg. You're a bad egg... You've derailed this meeting with another obscure comment.

jokes.text: Murray vs. Maxwell (Australia) stall competition: 'We've got rocks.' 'Massive.' 'We've got a big... smaller rocks.'
jokes.setup: Murray is gloating to his Australian counterpart Maxwell that New Zealand's band will beat Australia's entertainment.
jokes.punchline: Maxwell: 'Got Ayer's Rock, mate. Big huge rock.' Murray: 'Yeah, we've got rocks.' Maxwell: 'Massive.' Murray: '...We've got a big... smaller rocks.' The pause before 'smaller rocks' is the whole joke.
jokes.explanation: Exquisitely timed. Murray's competitive swagger deflates in real time as he's unable to match Ayers Rock with a comparable New Zealand rock. The hesitation before '...smaller rocks' — the visible moment where Murray searches for a counterclaim and comes up with only a qualified concession — is both perfectly written and dependent on the actor's timing. The specificity ('a big... smaller rocks') is superior to any generic comeback. Justifies the 8+ — it's economically perfect and gets funnier the more you think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You'd expect three sauces for that, wouldn't you?

jokes.text: Murray is forced to perform with just Jemaine and a cassette tape — pressing play while Maxwell watches
jokes.setup: Bret hasn't shown up. Murray has promised the band will 'blow the Aussies out of the water.' The tape is their only option.
jokes.punchline: Murray fumbles with the cassette player trying to find the right spot on the tape ('I have to rewind it') while Maxwell watches with growing amusement. Maxwell: 'Is this your band, Murray? A guy and a tape?' Murray: 'Classic.' Then 'Drop the beat.' 'I'm trying, man.'
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the cassette tape setup is well-executed cringe. Murray's 'Classic' responding to Maxwell's mockery — presumably trying to spin the tape as a deliberate artistic choice — is desperate and funny. The fumbling with the cassette rewind while the Australians have 'Wet N Wild Welcome Girls' is the Australia competition joke reaching its nadir.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: People who hold signs go on to hold many things

jokes.text: SONG (tape performance): 'Inner City Pressure' / 'Shake your boobies yeah / Who likes to rock the party? / All the ladies with the babies / Make your babies shake their booties yeah'
jokes.setup: The 'Boom' song debate ('booties' vs. 'boobies') has been resolved: the tape uses both ('shake your boobies yeah' and 'make your babies shake their booties yeah'). The Jemaine-and-tape performance begins.
jokes.punchline: The song being performed alone to a Trade Expo crowd by one man and a cassette player is inherently absurd. The song itself resolves the booties/boobies debate by including both — the babies-with-booties interpretation made literal ('all the ladies with the babies / make your babies shake their booties yeah').
jokes.explanation: This is a convergence point for multiple setups: the booties/boobies debate is resolved (in the most absurd way — by including both, with babies), the tape is being performed at an actual gig, and Jemaine's earnest hip-hop aspirations are played straight. The fact that the crowd apparently enjoys it ('Awesome.' 'Yes!') deepens the joke. The episode earns this moment.

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: Brit like... like Brittany?

jokes.text: 'Blew the Aussies right out of the water. The tape was great.'
jokes.setup: Murray had boasted about blowing the Aussies away. The band performed as a guy and a cassette.
jokes.punchline: Murray declares total victory, specifically crediting the tape. His triumph is real and unironic — he genuinely considers this a win. The tape outperformed Bret.
jokes.explanation: The double irony — the tape won AND the Aussies were somehow beaten — pays off both setups simultaneously. Murray's sincere pride in the tape is the best version of his character: he adapts his standards to whatever actually happened and declares it a success.

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: There's Vikings there, right?

jokes.text: Murray debates firing Bret, Jemaine accidentally reveals the bluff ('He told me he was going to pretend to fire you if you were late'), then Murray declares 'I've turned around' and fires Bret for real
jokes.setup: Murray says he has 'a right mind to fire you, Bret.' Bret calls him on it: 'You're bluffing.' Murray insists he's not. Then Jemaine, trying to defend Murray, reveals the bluff.
jokes.punchline: 'He told me he was going to pretend to fire you if you were late.' Murray: 'Good one. Yeah, I was going to.' Bret: 'So you were bluffing?' Murray: 'Yeah, but I'm not now. This is the whole... I've turned around.' Then actually fires Bret. Then fires Jemaine from the car because he ruined the bluff.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully structured escalation. The three-act mini-structure — bluff, bluff revealed, bluff becomes real — is executed with perfect timing. Murray's 'I've turned around' as the logic for now genuinely firing Bret is delightful managerial logic. The kicker — firing Jemaine not for anything band-related but specifically for 'ruining the bluff' — is character-perfect Murray: personal pride in his authority takes precedence over all other concerns. Justifies 8+ on structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's so hot she's making me sexist. Bitch.

jokes.text: Jemaine blows Murray's bluff immediately — 'He told me he was going to pretend to fire you if you were late'
jokes.setup: Murray is building to a dramatic firing
jokes.punchline: Jemaine casually undermines the entire maneuver; Murray then pivots to 'I'm not bluffing now. I've turned around.'
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's completely neutral betrayal of Murray's scheme — with no malice, just helpfully clarifying — and Murray's sincere pivot ('I've turned around') is excellent character comedy. The logic of becoming genuinely angry because your fake anger was exposed is perfectly Murray. Justifies 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Boom King song - entire performance

jokes.text: Mel's consolation speech: 'You really know how to move that sign. You just manipulate it and caress it with your skillful hands.'
jokes.setup: Bret has been fired and is at his lowest point. Mel, the band's only fan, tries to comfort him.
jokes.punchline: Mel's only source of comfort for Bret is the sign-holding compliment — which she delivers with an intensity that crosses into uncomfortable devotion: 'manipulate it and caress it with your skillful hands.' Her enthusiasm for the sign work is proportionally greater than her interest in the band.
jokes.explanation: Mel is the show's best recurring cringe character — her low-level obsession with the band (particularly Jemaine) is always slightly off in a way that makes her sympathy feel like surveillance. 'Manipulate and caress' applied to a sign-holding shift is the right register of uncomfortable. Character-integrated: only Mel would find this meaningfully consoling.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Imagine that, a whole fence made out of toothbrushes. Over 50 toothbrushes.

jokes.text: Mel describes watching Bret hold signs: 'You really know how to move that sign. You just manipulate it and caress it with your skillful hands.'
jokes.setup: Mel is consoling Bret about being fired from the band
jokes.punchline: Her description of watching him hold signs is sensual and uncomfortable — she's been watching him at work, she's clearly attracted, and the language is out of proportion to the activity
jokes.explanation: Mel's character — obsessive fan with no social awareness — is perfectly expressed here. The specific language ('caress it with your skillful hands') applied to sign-holding is wonderfully wrong. Character integration is very high — this could only be Mel. Justifies 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Maxwell
dialogue.line: Got Ayer's Rock, mate. Big huge rock.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We've got a big... smaller rocks.

jokes.text: 'Way better than those Australian girls in their bikinis.' / 'I didn't like them.' / 'Oh, I liked them.' / 'I hated them.'
jokes.setup: Mel praises Jemaine's performance as better than the Australian bikini girls.
jokes.punchline: Mel: 'I didn't like them.' Jemaine: 'Oh, I liked them.' Mel: 'I hated them.' The rapid escalation of Mel's dislike in direct proportion to Jemaine's admission of liking them reveals the jealousy beneath the compliment. Doug says almost nothing throughout.
jokes.explanation: Short and sharp. The pivot from 'I didn't like them' to 'I hated them' in two beats based purely on Jemaine's admission is a clean character beat for Mel. Doug's functional non-presence in the car scenes is its own running joke.

dialogue.speaker: Maxwell
dialogue.line: Is this your band, Murray? A guy and a tape? Classic.

jokes.text: A booking agent saw the tape-and-Jemaine act and wants to book them — Bret is excluded from this gig
jokes.setup: Bret has just been fired from the band he helped found. Jemaine mentions they got a real gig.
jokes.punchline: The gig that resulted from Bret's absence is the first real gig they've gotten all episode. The tape that replaced him directly caused their first booking success. This is structural irony sustained over the entire episode.
jokes.explanation: A narrative joke — the payoff lands at the episode level. The tape replacing Bret has functionally improved the band's career, which is the cruelest possible outcome for Bret. Works best on rewatch when the full structural irony is visible.

dialogue.line: Song lyrics substituting 'boobies' for 'booties' and babies shaking booties

jokes.text: 'The car could just burst into flames right now and this would be the way to go, huh, guys?' — Bret asks to be let out immediately
jokes.setup: Bret has been fired and the car ride with the happy couple (Mel and Doug) is becoming unbearable.
jokes.punchline: Bret wistfully announces that a car fire would be a good death right now, then immediately asks to be dropped off: 'Just here, thanks, Doug.' The speed of the transition from existential despair to practical logistics is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the Conchords' typical treatment of profound misery as a mild inconvenience — Bret going from 'I want to die in a car fire' to 'actually just drop me here' without tonal shift. The New Zealand emotional understatement applied to career devastation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There's very little difference between you and the tape, I noticed.

jokes.text: Murray and Jemaine's 'tape vs. Bret' comparison scorecard: 'More compact.' / 'I can carry the tape.' / 'You could carry Bret. How much can you press?' / 'Not to a gig.'
jokes.setup: Murray and Jemaine are formally assessing whether the tape is a better bandmate than Bret.
jokes.punchline: Murray raises 'more compact' as a pro-tape point. Jemaine challenges: 'You could carry Bret.' Murray: 'Not to a gig. I couldn't carry him to a gig.' The idea that physically transporting Bret is a solved problem except for the gig logistics is treated as a genuine comparative analysis.
jokes.explanation: The formality of the comparison — treating Bret's physical mass and portability as genuine variables in a musician evaluation — is both absurd and somehow logical within the episode's world. Murray's practical concerns about carrying Bret TO a gig specifically (implying he could carry him elsewhere) is the right level of weird.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're fired.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Wait, Murray, that's not what we planned.

jokes.text: Murray asks if Jemaine misses Bret. Jemaine: 'Not really.' Murray: 'You miss him?' Jemaine: 'A little bit.' Murray: 'I might put that down as a... thing.'
jokes.setup: The tape-vs.-Bret evaluation concludes. Murray asks the emotional question.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's admission of missing Bret 'a little bit' is immediately bureaucratized by Murray: 'I might put that down as a... thing.' He is literally adding 'missing Bret' to some kind of official document.
jokes.explanation: Murray converting a genuine emotional admission into a bureaucratic line item ('put that down as a... thing') is character perfection. The hesitation before 'thing' — even Murray doesn't know what category this falls under — is the right note.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, 'cause you ruined my bluff. The whole bluff thing.

jokes.text: 'Don't stand next to any big magnets.' / 'Why would I stand next to big magnets, Murray?' / 'I don't know what you do in your personal life.'
jokes.setup: Murray warns Jemaine to protect the cassette tape — their only remaining bandmate.
jokes.punchline: Murray's warning escalates to 'don't stand next to big magnets.' When Jemaine challenges this as a bizarre concern, Murray: 'I don't know what you do in your personal life.' The implication that Jemaine's life might plausibly involve large magnets is absurd, and Murray's non-judgmental acknowledgment of that possibility is perfect.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines and fully justifies 8+. 'I don't know what you do in your personal life' is the punchline, but it's also a worldbuilding statement — Murray's complete ignorance of and incuriosity about his artists as human beings is the joke. The line is structured perfectly: the odd warning → the reasonable challenge → the non-answer that's somehow more absurd than the warning. Highly quotable outside context. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in economy and character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: All your fans are gonna be devastated.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's just you, Mel.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: The tape gets caught on Jemaine's zipper — the band is dissolved for the second time
jokes.setup: Murray asks to see the tape. Jemaine goes to show it and it catches on his zipper.
jokes.punchline: The tape unspools in Jemaine's pocket. Murray has a 'trick from the '80s' to fix it. It doesn't work. 'Does this mean I'm not in a band anymore?' 'I'm afraid not, Jemaine.'
jokes.explanation: The tape's destruction is a perfect structural comedy payoff. Murray spent an entire scene warning Jemaine about magnets, and the tape is destroyed by a zipper — the mundane domestic hazard, not the exotic one he warned about. Jemaine asking 'Does this mean I'm not in a band anymore?' treats the tape as a genuine bandmate with the same weight as Bret's firing. Murray's formal 'I'm afraid not, Jemaine' is the ideal institutional response to a cassette being mangled. The 'trick from the '80s' failing seals it.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mel's sexual description of Bret's sign skills

jokes.text: 'Greg, could we get a couple of tissues in here? Greg? ... Go away, Greg.' / 'Sue them. Just sue them.'
jokes.setup: Murray and Jemaine are in genuine distress about the tape. Murray asks Greg for tissues.
jokes.punchline: Greg apparently enters immediately ('Go away, Greg'), then someone (Greg?) offers advice: 'Sue them. Just sue them.' Murray: 'Oh... oh, I got to go.' The non sequitur 'Sue them' and Murray's abrupt exit is the right level of weird.
jokes.explanation: This is a minor beat — it exists mainly to close out the tape-destruction scene and remind us Murray is in an office. 'Sue them' is funny as a completely irrelevant suggestion to a zipper accident. 'Just sue them' — the emphasis on 'just' implying this is obvious — is the best part.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: So, when's your next gig?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, we've got a big one coming up. Really big. Corporate event. They're flying us out, putting us up in a hotel.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Really? That's great!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's gonna be huge.

jokes.text: Bret has a Bluetooth earpiece that neither he nor Jemaine can identify or explain
jokes.setup: Bret has adopted the trappings of a business career. He has a Bluetooth earpiece as a status symbol.
jokes.punchline: 'It's my Bluetooth brand. Everyone has 'em around here. It's a business thing.' Jemaine: 'You don't know what it is, do you?' Bret: 'Not really. Do you know what it is?' Jemaine: 'No. I think you use it with the phone.' Bret: 'Oh right, I see. Yeah yeah, I see. Yeah yeah, that makes sense.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat — Bret has adopted corporate signifiers he doesn't understand because he wants to belong to this new world. Neither man knows what a Bluetooth earpiece does, but both perform comprehension at the end ('Yeah yeah, that makes sense'). The mutual ignorance and mutual performance of understanding is very FotC.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: The car could just burst into flames right now and this would be the way to go

jokes.text: Bret's 'I've grown up' speech: 'I've changed. I've grown up. I've got a job. I've got a career. I drink coffee now.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Bret to rejoin the band. Bret resists, explaining he's moved on.
jokes.punchline: The evidence of his maturity: a job, a career, and coffee. The coffee is the punchline — the smallest possible signifier of adult identity offered as the culminating proof of his transformation. Then he drinks it and visibly hates it.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The escalation — job, career, COFFEE — builds to the most trivial possible proof of adulthood, and Bret's visible disgust at the coffee immediately following undercuts even that. The visual gag (drinking the coffee and grimacing) makes the text joke perfect: he can't even perform the adulthood he's claiming. 'I drink coffee now' as an identity statement is quotable and character-specific in a way that justifies 8+. The structure (list that deflates to bathetic conclusion) is clean.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Don't stand next to any big magnets.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Why would I stand next to big magnets, Murray?

jokes.text: Jemaine's recruitment pitch: 'Free phones.' Bret immediately says yes.
jokes.setup: Bret has been firmly refusing to rejoin the band through multiple requests. Jemaine has no compelling argument.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine points to a 'Free Phones' sign: 'Free phones.' Bret's entire career-identity speech dissolves in one second. The next scene is him running through the streets screaming 'I'm back in the band!'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's second-best comedic moment and the most structurally satisfying. Everything that came before — Bret's identity crisis, his 'I've grown up' speech, the coffee — is punctured by 'free phones.' Not a compelling argument, not an emotional appeal, not music calling him back: a promotional offer for free phones. The economy is perfect: two words destroy all prior character development and resolve the episode. Character integration is exceptional: Bret's susceptibility to this is completely believable given who he is. The cut to him running through the streets euphorically yelling 'I'm back in the band!' is the visual payoff.

dialogue.line: Tape getting tangled in zipper and Murray's '80s fix attempt

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'You've given up the dream, Bret!' / Bret: 'I've woken up.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine dramatically accuses Bret of abandoning his artistic dream
jokes.punchline: Bret's comeback — 'I've woken up' — is simultaneously a genuine rebuttal and completely undermined by having just been talked into rejoining for a phone
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on 'dream' (giving up vs. waking up) is excellent — it's the episode's title made literal. The dramatic delivery of 'I've woken up' as a philosophical statement of hard-won realism, combined with the immediate capitulation for a free phone, is the episode's thematic joke. Highly quotable. Justifies 8.3.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Does this mean I'm not in a band anymore?

jokes.text: Jemaine on his phone while Bret has just been fired: 'Yeah, well I'll see what I can do. Yeah. I've got a 5:00 and a 6:00. I'll pencil you in.'
jokes.setup: Bret is in existential crisis about the band; Jemaine is on the phone
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is doing a business-person impression on the phone, using scheduling language ('I'll pencil you in') — exactly mimicking Bret's affected professionalism
jokes.explanation: Jemaine adopting the same corporate persona as Bret while Bret is in crisis is a nice ironic mirror. The scene shows Jemaine is also susceptible to the same delusions. Decent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I've grown up. I've got a job. I've got a career. I drink coffee now.

jokes.text: Bret tells Coco he quit the band voluntarily and is now 'concentrating on signs' as 'bigger and better things'
jokes.setup: Bret is fired from the band and is maintaining his dignity with Coco.
jokes.punchline: 'They wanted me back but I'm just concentrating on signs and my career.' Coco: 'Signs?' Bret: 'Yeah.' Coco: 'I thought you loved signs?' Bret: 'Signs are okay.' Coco: 'Yeah.' Bret: 'I prefer bands.' The entire constructed narrative of 'bigger and better things' collapses in about 20 seconds under gentle questioning.
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure: Bret builds a confident facade ('bigger and better things / signs') and Coco, without any intention of deflating it, simply asks 'Signs?' The entire thing collapses not through confrontation but through the mildest possible skepticism. 'I prefer bands' — his honest admission — delivered flatly after the entire speech is a well-earned beat. The economy of the collapse is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh God, yuck.

jokes.text: Bret runs through the streets yelling 'I'm back in the band!' to strangers, culminating in 'Put the cat down. We can't afford it.'
jokes.setup: Bret has rejoined the band and is overwhelmed with joy.
jokes.punchline: He runs through the streets telling everyone he's back in the band. Someone is apparently holding a cat — possibly intending to buy or adopt it — and Bret's new fiscal awareness kicks in: 'Put the cat down. We can't afford it.' The poverty of their lifestyle has not changed at all.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the episode's opening poverty theme is satisfying — nothing has changed, they are still too poor for a cat — but wrapped in Bret's euphoric joy. The mundane correction ('we can't afford it') delivered mid-celebration is the right comic note. The visual of him running through streets as a full Rocky-style comeback is enhanced by how small the 'comeback' actually is.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You've given up the dream, Bret!

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I've woken up.

jokes.text: Murray holds auditions to replace Bret, selects 'Brent' — 'It's not about talent. You're not the talented one. But with one letter difference, it's quite easy to do the transition.'
jokes.setup: Murray, having fired Bret, is holding formal auditions to find his replacement.
jokes.punchline: Murray eliminates a talented applicant ('Josh... high scores but a little arrogant') in favor of Brent, who apparently has Bret's look and a one-letter-different name. 'It's not about talent. You're not the talented one. But you do look very similar.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's criteria for band membership (looks similar to Bret + name is one letter off) as more important than talent is absurdist management comedy at its peak. The dismissal of Josh — 'high scores but a little arrogant' — implies Murray has a structured evaluation rubric for sign holders/band members that includes 'arrogance' as a demerit. Telling Brent 'you're not the talented one' to his face as if it's fine is peak Murray: he never considers the emotional impact of his communication.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought you'd prefer the signs.

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: No, I prefer bands.

jokes.text: Murray holds auditions for Bret's replacement — 'It's not about talent. It's about the look.' — gives role to Brent
jokes.setup: Murray has rapidly moved on to replacing Bret
jokes.punchline: He has conducted formal auditions; the winner is 'Brent' because of the visual similarity and one-letter name difference ('quite easy to just do the transition there')
jokes.explanation: Murray's criteria — not talent, just similar appearance and near-identical name — is perfect Murray logic. 'With one letter difference, it's quite easy to just do the transition there' suggests Murray was already planning to simply not tell anyone. Character integration is very high. Justifies 8.0.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: It's not about talent. You're not the talented one. But you do look very similar.

jokes.text: Eddie made the band a sign: 'FOTC' — 'OH, NO THAT ONE. THAT ONE.' — The sign reads 'Hot Dogs'
jokes.setup: Eddie, the sign boss, has made a supportive sign for the band. Murray's excited reaction suggests it's impressive.
jokes.punchline: It's revealed to be a 'Hot Dogs' sign — i.e., a work sign Eddie had on hand. The 'OH NO THAT ONE. THAT ONE.' (Eddie misdirecting them to a different sign) implies there IS an FOTC sign, but what they're shown first is the hot dog advertisement. The show may be implying the FOTC sign is similar in quality.
jokes.explanation: A decent visual callback connecting Eddie's sign world to the band — his support takes the form of professional sign-making because that's his domain. The accidental 'Hot Dogs' reveal is a mild gag. The transcript suggests the actual FOTC sign is shown after, making 'wow, that's fantastic' the real button if the sign is also low-quality, but this is ambiguous.

jokes.text: Murray casually fires Brent ('sorry, we'll keep your number on file') after hiring him minutes earlier when Bret returns
jokes.setup: Murray has just enthusiastically hired Brent
jokes.punchline: He dismisses Brent with HR boilerplate ('keep your number on file') without a moment's hesitation when the original Bret appears
jokes.explanation: The HR language used to dismiss someone hired 60 seconds ago is a good Murray beat. The show's confidence in quickly resolving Brent to maintain status quo is well-handled.

jokes.text: Murray and Jemaine jointly confirm they missed Bret — 'Both... we both missed you.' / 'No, a little bit.' / 'You could see that you were...' — then immediately cut to unceremoniously firing Brent
jokes.setup: Bret returns and is welcomed back. There's a brief emotional moment about being missed.
jokes.punchline: The emotional reconciliation lasts about 15 seconds before Murray pivots to: 'Oh, bad news, guys. This is Bret. He's gonna take the position. So yeah, that's it, I guess. Brent... sorry. We'll keep your number on file.'
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'we'll keep your number on file' to Brent — the standard HR dismissal applied to a musician who was the band's entire lineup an hour ago — is good institutional comedy. The speed of the pivot from emotional reunion to cold HR language is perfect Murray. It also resolves the 'tape vs. Bret' and 'Brent vs. Bret' threads simultaneously.

jokes.text: Jemaine claims he didn't miss Bret — Murray and Bret both call him out — 'No, a little bit'
jokes.setup: Bret returns to the band
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's denial of missing Bret — established earlier — is directly recalled and he is again forced to admit 'a little bit'
jokes.explanation: The callback completes the emotional arc — Jemaine can't maintain his indifference when Bret is literally in front of him. The 'a little bit' is a good consistent character note.

tmdb_overview: Faced with dire financial circumstances, Bret and Jemaine look for jobs. Bret gets a job holding signs but soon he has no time for the band and Murray and Jemaine start looking for a replacement.
